creative_brief: Hero image: a parent and child at the Brookvale hub, paper bag in hand, with a handwritten farm name visible on the label. Warm, natural light. Below the fold, a simple visual map showing the direct line from named farms to the hub — no warehouses in between. Earthy palette, no stock photography. Every image should feel like a Saturday morning, not a brand shoot.
headline: I didn't know the name of a single farmer who fed my kids
subhead: Not one. After years of filling the trolley on autopilot, I couldn't tell you where a single egg, cut of meat, or handful of greens actually came from. FarmThru exists so that never has to be true for your family again.
hero_copy: Here's what I'm embarrassed to admit. I cared about what my kids ate — or thought I did. I checked labels. I chose 'free range.' I paid more for 'locally grown.' But when my daughter asked me who grew her carrots, I had nothing. No name. No farm. No story. Just a barcode and a best guess. That silence changed everything for me. I started tracing the supply chain — past the wholesalers, past the warehouses, past the cold storage — and found farmers like Rachel Ward in Kempsey, NSW, who'd been growing regenerative food for years with almost no way to reach families like mine.

sections.heading: The grocery system wasn't built for you to ask questions
sections.body:
Your supermarket pork passes through warehouses and wholesalers before it reaches the shelf. By the time you pick it up, nobody can tell you which farm it came from or how the animal was raised. That's not a bug — it's the design. Opacity keeps margins high and questions low.

FarmThru strips all of that out. Zero warehouses. Zero wholesalers. Zero middlemen. We connect you directly to farms like Bundarra (pork belly, fennel salami, Little French Ham), Collins Wild Caught (wild-caught seafood from the South Coast), Farmer Brown (eggs), and Paris Creek Farms (carbon-neutral biodynamic dairy from South Australia). Around 15–20 partner farms and suppliers across NSW and beyond — and we'll show you where your food comes from. Every product. Every time.

sections.heading: How it actually works (it's simpler than your current shop)
sections.body:
FarmThru is a farm-direct grocery store, not a subscription box. No lock-in. No membership fees. You order online when you want, what you want. Then you collect from our Brookvale hub — Unit 23, 10–18 Orchard Rd — Monday to Friday. That's it.

Because we cut the middlemen, food moves from farm to hub to you. It's fresher. Farmers get paid fairly. And bulk buying means you save more when you commit to the things your family already eats every week. No commitment required — but the option rewards you if you do.

sections.heading: This isn't just a grocery store. It's a bet on a different food system.
sections.body:
We're opening up the chance for everyday people to own a piece of FarmThru through our equity crowdfunding campaign. Not as customers — as partners.

You can join the waitlist now with a fully refundable $5 deposit. No commitment beyond that. You'll be first to know when the campaign goes live, and you'll have the chance to vote for change with your wallet — not just by buying better food, but by backing the infrastructure that makes it possible.

This is early. It carries real risk. There are no guaranteed returns, and like any investment, you could lose what you put in. We won't pretend otherwise. But if you've ever stood in a supermarket aisle and felt that quiet frustration — the sense that this system wasn't built for your family — this is your chance to be part of building something that is.

sections.heading: Compliance
sections.body: FarmThru is preparing an equity crowdfunding campaign. Any investment carries risk, including the potential loss of your entire investment. Past performance is not a reliable indicator of future results. No returns are guaranteed. This page is not financial advice. Please read the offer document carefully before making any investment decision. The $5 deposit to join the waitlist is fully refundable at any time.
